questions.scenario: The public DNS record resolves, Application Gateway receives traffic, and backend health shows one API copy unhealthy. What is the smallest useful first action?

options.text: Delete and recreate the public DNS record.
Inspect the unhealthy backend copy and keep the healthy copy serving traffic.
Open the API subnet to the Internet to remove possible NSG issues.
Change the SQL private endpoint because all checkout failures eventually touch SQL.

questions.explanation: The evidence has reached backend health. The first action should stay at that boundary and avoid changing DNS, SQL, or broad access rules.

questions.scenario: The app logs show SQL connection timeouts. The private endpoint exists, but the private DNS record points at an old IP. What fix direction matches the evidence?

options.text: Update or repair the private DNS record for the SQL private endpoint.
Increase the app timeout so users wait longer.
Grant the app broader Azure RBAC.

questions.explanation: The failure is a name-to-private-IP mismatch. More retries, broad RBAC, or address-space replacement do not address the named boundary.

questions.scenario: Which changes should make the team pause because they are broader than the evidence?

options.text: Changing an NSG rule for every subnet when only the API subnet is affected.
Repairing one private DNS record that is proven stale.
Replacing the whole VNet during a checkout incident.
Adding a public SQL endpoint to bypass the private endpoint.

questions.explanation: Broad network changes can create new exposure or downtime. A proven stale DNS record is narrow and directly tied to the evidence.
